ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal kangkong with squid. FOODON_03311737 (stir-fried vegetable dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Vegetables, stir-fried) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for sambal kangkong with squid in the FoodEx2 catalogue.

main_ingredients: sambal paste (dried chillies, shrimp paste / belacan, garlic, shallots)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sambal kangkong with squid in Singapore is approximately 300 g, shared among 2–4 diners as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Kangkong (Water Spinach), stir-fried with sambal and seafood — estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo component data: Kangkong stir-fried (HPB ID: F0085) and Squid, cooked (HPB ID: F0312), combined with sambal paste contribution
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for sambal kangkong with squid as a combined dish. Values estimated by weighted combination: approximately 60% kangkong stir-fried (HPB F0085), 25% cooked squid (HPB F0312), and 15% sambal paste contribution (HPB F0421 / chilli-based condiment). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for water spinach (FDC ID 168430) and squid (FDC ID 175168). All values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and seafood stir-fry dishes — analogous estimate based on water spinach (kangkong) GI and low-carbohydrate seafood contribution; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal kangkong with squid. GI estimated at 32 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being kangkong (water spinach), a non-starchy leafy vegetable with a low GI (estimated ~32 by analogy with other non-starchy leafy vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). Squid and sambal paste contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 14.4) / 100 = 4.6, where serving carbohydrate = 4.8 g/100g × 300 g serving = 14.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal paste and shrimp paste (belacan)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of sambal kangkong with squid due to its elevated sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g, approximately 1260 mg per restaurant serving), which can contribute significantly to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal kangkong with squid has a low glycemic index (~32) and low carbohydrate content (~4.8 g per 100 g), making it a suitable vegetable-seafood dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; however, sodium from sambal paste should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from squid
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from sambal paste and belacan, and the moderate cholesterol contribution from squid (~95 mg per 100 g); the dish provides beneficial micronutrients including potassium, vitamin C, and iron from kangkong, but portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal kangkong with squid (鲜鱿叁巴雍菜) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood-vegetable stir-fry dish consisting of kangkong (water spinach, Ipomoea aquatica) cooked in a spicy sambal paste together with fresh squid. The dish is prepared by stir-frying kangkong and squid in a wok over high heat with sambal paste made from dried chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), garlic, and shallots, resulting in a dish that is intensely aromatic, spicy, and savoury with a slight smokiness from the wok. The kangkong provides a tender yet slightly crunchy texture, while the squid adds a chewy, protein-rich element. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in carbohydrates (~4.8 g per 100 g) and has a low glycemic index (~32), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It provides moderate protein (~7.2 g per 100 g) primarily from squid, and is a good source of vitamin A, vitamin C, iron, and calcium from the kangkong. The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the sambal paste and belacan, which is a clinical consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly served as a shared side dish in Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) establishments, typically priced at SGD 18–28 per plate. It is halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

meal_context: shared side dish in a multi-dish Chinese meal

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal kangkong with squid as a combined dish in HPB Foodo or any other primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component combination using HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried kangkong and cooked squid, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI profile of non-starchy leafy vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component data (HPB Foodo stir-fried kangkong + cooked squid + sambal paste contribution) as no single combined dish entry exists in an approved primary source. Values should be updated if a direct HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo component entries for kangkong or squid, and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the traditional preparation of this dish using vegetable cooking oil. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~32) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being non-starchy leafy kangkong; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Vegetables', which more accurately reflects the primary ingredient (kangkong) and the nature of the dish. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and other micronutrients are estimated from component HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central data and carry medium confidence. 7. Allergen note: belacan (shrimp paste) contains crustaceans; squid is a mollusc — both are major allergens.
